coredata.dc:description: This study focuses on preparing Carbon dots (CDs) from Pyrene-1-carbaldehyde (PCA) using a solvothermal method and further purification using column chromatography. The aggregation-induced emission (AIE) of CDs was systematically investigated in a THF/water medium. The CDs showed red shifts in their photoluminescence (PL) spectra upon increase in water content. Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images revealed the formation of aggregates, while X-ray diffraction (XRD) confirmed that the d-spacing values remains unchanged. The NMR spectrum of the CDs displayed peaks corresponding to aromatic carbon, which disappeared upon addition of water due to π-π stacking, indicating aggregate formation. Based on the aggregation-induced fluorescence emission mechanism, detection of water content in alcohol is demonstrated. Moreover, the synthesized CDs were used as fluorescent colorant in screen inks along with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) as binders. The print proofs obtained on UV-dull paper using PVA-based screen ink exhibited fluorescence emission at longer wavelengths and showcased desirable photostability under prolonged UV exposure compared to the prints obtained using HEC-based ink. Moreover, though the PVA based print appeared blue or cyan fluorescent, the actual yellow emissions from the CDs can be visualised using UV block filter. Such features, masked to the forger, but known to the user can be utilised in checking the authenticity of the print.

head.ce:title: Pyrene carbaldehyde derived carbon dots for detecting water in alcohol and security printing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study focuses on preparing Carbon dots (CDs) from Pyrene-1-carbaldehyde (PCA) using a solvothermal method and further purification using column chromatography. The aggregation-induced emission (AIE) of CDs was systematically investigated in a THF/water medium. The CDs showed red shifts in their photoluminescence (PL) spectra upon increase in water content. Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images revealed the formation of aggregates, while X-ray diffraction (XRD) confirmed that the d-spacing values remains unchanged. The NMR spectrum of the CDs displayed peaks corresponding to aromatic carbon, which disappeared upon addition of water due to π-π stacking, indicating aggregate formation. Based on the aggregation-induced fluorescence emission mechanism, detection of water content in alcohol is demonstrated. Moreover, the synthesized CDs were used as fluorescent colorant in screen inks along with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) as binders. The print proofs obtained on UV-dull paper using PVA-based screen ink exhibited fluorescence emission at longer wavelengths and showcased desirable photostability under prolonged UV exposure compared to the prints obtained using HEC-based ink. Moreover, though the PVA based print appeared blue or cyan fluorescent, the actual yellow emissions from the CDs can be visualised using UV block filter. Such features, masked to the forger, but known to the user can be utilised in checking the authenticity of the print.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimension nanoparticles belonging to an extensively studied group of carbon nanomaterials. Their popularity results from facile synthesis, availability of diverse precursor materials, lesser cytotoxicity, and biocompatibility [1–3]. In 2004 after their serendipitous discovery along with carbon nanotubes, they were identified as fluorescent carbon nanoparticles [4]. There are several mechanisms which explicitly mention the origin of fluorescence in CDs, which is governed by size dependent bandgap (quantum confinement) due to extensive crosslinked sp2 carbon backbone and surface passivation effects related to surface defects which arise because of several functional groups linked to sp2 carbon backbone [5]. In addition, tunable photoluminescence (PL) can be achieved either by controlling the sp2 carbon of the core or modification of surface groups [6,7]. Despite remarkable fluorescence in liquid state, CDs suffer from aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) in solid state due to excessive π-π resonance transfer [8]. Therefore, attempts were made to disperse CDs in solid matrices like silica, starch, salt and polymer [9–11]. Another phenomenon wherein the CDs are least emissive in dispersive state and highly fluorescent in aggregate state is termed as aggregation induced emission (AIE) [12]. Yang and group synthesized hydrophobic CDs with pyridinic and epoxy groups through one-pot solvothermal treatment of melamine and dithiosalicylic acid, which displayed AIE [13]. The CDs exhibited blue emission (λem = 467 nm) when dispersed in ethanol but displayed remarkable red shift (λem = 621 nm) on addition of water. Mao and team prepared hydrophobic CDs from urea and 2,2-dithiosalicylic acid via hydrothermal approach [14]. On UV illumination, the CDs in dispersed state displayed blue emission (λem = 475 nm) and in aggregated state they exhibited orange-red fluorescence (λem = 618 nm). The free rotation of disulfide bond is hindered due to π-π stacking in aggregated state.

The present study involves the synthesis of CDs from Pyrene-1-carbaldehyde (PCA) by solvothermal method. The CDs exhibit green fluorescence (λem = 535 nm) in dispersion state with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 13.48 %. The phenomenon of AIE in CDs was realised when dispersed in water medium with red shift (λem = 560 nm) in the PL spectra and visible settlement of aggregates. Lee and the research team reported PL quenching of CDs upon addition of water. This property served in detection of water adulteration in ethanol [15]. Another research group demonstrated the use of CDs in detection of water in THF samples via spectroscopic and colorimetric technique [16]. However, Hu and team found that CDs prepared from Cordyceps militaris exhibited decline in QY with addition of water. Hence, they served as fluorescent probes for analysis of alcoholic content in beverage samples [17]. Therefore, the PCA-derived CDs were explored for detecting water content in alcohol.

The emergence and further rise of counterfeit products has led to significant financial losses and health hazards. Various anti-counterfeit techniques have been developed to combat this, including security inks with distinct features. Invisible security inks are remarkable as they are not perceptible to the naked eye but display unique covert fluorescence features under various light sources. Hence, the PCA-derived CDs are used to formulate inks incorporating two different binders: polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) for screen printing. The photo-stability and abrasion resistance of the printed samples are evaluated. The PVA based security ink demonstrated better print features and desirable anti-counterfeit features in comparison to HEC based formulation. The security inks with characteristic emissions can be used to store information which can be checked for the authenticity by the user.

About 82 mg of PCA was dissolved in ethanol, the contents made up to 10 ML using the same solvent and transferred into a Teflon coated autoclave. The solvothermal reaction was performed at an optimized temperature of 150℃ for 1, 3 and 5 h and the resultant mother liquors (ML-1, ML-3 and ML-5) were filtered to remove any suspended particles. Column (150 mm length, 35 ML capacity) for chromatographic separation was prepared by wet packing method using silica gel (400–700 meshwork) and DCM was loaded to separate the CDs of interest from MLs.

The absorption measurements were conducted using a Schimadzu spectrophotometer. The emission spectra were recorded using a JASCO spectrofluorometer FP-8300 equipment with the excitation source and emission bandwidths set between 2.5 and 5 nm. The QYs were measured by absolute method using an integrating sphere coated with BaSO4 attached to the spectrofluorometer for excitation and emission bandwidth set between 2.5 and 5 nm. The LC-MS analysis was performed on ACQUITY UPLC H-Class PLUS Bio System. About 0.1 ML sample was injected into a ACQUITY UPLC® BEH C18 LC column with a mesh size of 1.7 µm. The binary solvent optimised for separation was water with 0.1 % formic acid (solvent A) and methanol (solvent B). The separation of components was enabled via UV detector followed by mass analysis via positive electrospray ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry. The 13C NMR and DEPT-135 spectra were acquired using a Bruker Ascend equipment at 100 MHz. The time resolved fluorescence spectra were recorded using DeltaFlex TCSPC instrument equipped with conventional PMT detector set with 13 ps per bin (channel) resolution and an instrument response function of ∼70 ps. TEM/HRTEM imaging and SAED patterns were obtained using JEM 2100 F model with a 200 kV electron source. X-ray diffraction studies were performed using a Rigaku ultima IV instrument with Cu kα as source at a scan rate of 2°/min and step size of 0.02. Elemental analysis was performed on Omicron XPS system with non-monochromatic Mg-Kα and Al-Kα as the excitation sources. Raman spectral data was acquired using a Renishaw Compact Raman Spectrometer with 785 nm excitation under 50X objective lens for 10 s exposure time. The characteristic functional groups were identified using an ATR-IR Shimadzu-IR Spirit analyzer with 40 scans at 4 cm−1 resolution. FESEM imaging was performed using Carl Zeiss High-Resolution Field Emission Gun Scanning Electron Microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PCA-CDs derived ink and evaluation of screen print properties
ce:section.ce:para: Two different screen inks were prepared using the PCA-CDs as colorant using two eco-friendly binders (industry grade): PVA and HEC. The inks were formulated by mixing the CDs in methanol with surfactant, defoamer and PVA/HEC. The thickness or grammage (in gsm) of UV dull paper used as substrate for screen printing was measured using Digital GSM tester. The screen print proofs were obtained by placing the UV dull paper beneath the screen mesh (mesh count 120) and ink was applied using the polyurethane squeeze at contact angle of 45°. The colorimetric and density values of the printed samples were estimated using Xrite i1 Pro spectrophotometer. The rub resistance of print proofs was tested using Sutherland rub tester by placing 2 lbs weight for 50 and 100 rubs.

Earlier studies report that pyrene derivatives when used as precursors confer desirable fluorescence properties to the CDs. Lan and group prepared highly fluorescent CDs from 1,3,6-trinitropyrene via hydrothermal approach, which displayed cyan fluorescence (λem = 490 nm) with a QY of 49 %. These were used as ‘on-off-on’ fluorescent probes to detect Fe3+ and dopamine in aqueous samples [18]. Another report states the simple heating of pyrene-1-butyric acid for the synthesis of CDs that showed enhanced fluorescence upon application of mechanical force (λem = 471 and 475 nm) [19]. In the present study, PCA (pyrene with an aldehyde group attached) that exhibits temperature and solvent dependent reversible tuneable fluorescence properties, long lifetime, and efficient formation of excimers [20] was used as the precursor. The MLs (mother liquor) obtained after solvothermal process for 1, 3 and 5 h were initially subjected to thin layer chromatography (TLC) before purification using column chromatography. Among the commonly used solvent systems to purify organic compounds including hexane:ethyl acetate, DCM (dichloromethane):methanol and petroleum ether:diethyl ether, DCM:methanol solvent system showed better separations and was suitably chosen to perform TLC (Supplementary Figure SF 1a-c) for ML-1, ML-3 and ML-5 to determine the number of constituent bands. The TLC separations for ML-1 were more pronounced for 9:1 DCM:methanol, 7:3 DCM:methanol, 5:5 DCM:methanol and 7:3 methanol:DCM elution systems. Whereas in case of ML-3, better separations of components were observed for 9:1 DCM:methanol, 7:3 DCM:methanol, 6:4 DCM:methanol and 7:3 methanol:DCM. For ML-5, TLC separations were observed when 9:1 DCM:methanol, 8:2 DCM:methanol, 6:4 DCM:methanol and 7:3 methanol:DCM solvent systems were chosen. Based on the better separations observed in TLC all three ML samples were subjected to column chromatography. The SF 2a-c depict the separation of different fractions upon increasing the polarity of methanol as solvent. The entire separation process for all the ML samples occurred in 2 h, wherein three fractions were eluted. The photographs of column purified fractions of ML-1, ML-3 and ML-5 under daylight and UV illumination are shown in SF 3a-f. Further, the absorbance spectrum of PCA in ethanol which showed peaks between 300 and 405 nm was compared with that of the MLs (SF 4a). After solvothermal treatment and subsequent column purification, the resultant MLs and respective eluted fractions exhibit absorbance almost analogous to that of PCA (SF 4b-d). This could indicate that similar chemical groups may be present in the product after solvothermal reaction.

PCA is a highly fluorescent molecule with characteristic solvatochromic properties. The 3D and 2D PL spectra of PCA were recorded in ethanol (SF 5a-b) and solid state (SF 5c-d). Ethanolic solution of PCA displays prominent emission at 450 nm and a weak emission at 530 nm for excitation at 430 nm. However, in its solid state the emission is observed at 550 nm mostly due to π-π stacking of pyrene moiety. Interestingly, the emission features are found to vary for MLs obtained after different durations of solvothermal reaction. The 3D PL spectrum of ML-1 depicts major emissions at 540 nm (marked as A) for corresponding excitation at 460 nm ( Fig. 1 a). However, it exhibits minor emission at 600 nm for excitation at 470 nm as shown in the 2D PL spectrum (Fig. 1 b). After column purification, the eluted fractions show unique emission properties. The first fraction possesses major emission at 430 and a minor emission at 530 nm for excitation at 407 nm (Fig. 1 c) indicating majority fluorophores emitting near UV region. The second fraction displays single PL peak at 580 nm for excitation at 470 nm (Fig. 1 d). In addition, emissions are observed at 490 and 570 nm for excitation at 440 nm (Fig. 1 e). This could indicate the presence of different types of fluorophores in the second fraction. The third fraction displays similar emission traits as ML-1 with emissions at 490 and 535 nm for excitation at 440 nm (Fig. 1 f). Moreover, it possesses minor emission at 620 nm for excitation at 470 nm (Fig. 1 g). The PL spectra were also recorded for ML-3 and ML-5 and its constituent fractions. The ML-3 display emissions at 490 and 540 nm for excitation at 460 nm (SF 6a). The first fraction shows emissions at 430 and 530 nm for excitation at 350 nm (SF 6b). However, the second and third fractions have similar emission region with major emissions at 450–460 nm for excitations at 430 and 434 nm (SF 6c-e). The PL spectrum of ML-5 depicts emissions at 490 and 540 nm for excitation at 466 nm (SF 7a). However, the first fraction possesses emissions at 470 and 530 nm for excitation at 418 nm indicating fluorophores in visible region (SF 7b). The latter two fractions show emissions at 490 and 530–535 nm for excitations at 440 and 446 nm, respectively (SF 7c-e). There is no shift in PL peak for third fraction of ML-3 and ML-5 for excitation at 470 nm. On the contrary, at same excitation, new fluorophores that emit at longer wavelength are observed in the third fraction of ML-1. We can thus speculate that solvothermal treatments for 3 h and beyond do not generate new fluorophores.

Further, to better comprehend the nature of fluorophores present in the third fraction, solvatochromism studies of third fraction of ML-1 and ML-5 were performed. The third fractions of ML-5 and ML-1 were dispersed in different solvents and viewed under daylight and 365 nm UV light (SF 8a-b and SF 8d-e). The third fraction of ML-5 when dispersed in aprotic and protic solvents does not exhibit major shift in PL spectra (SF 8c). Interestingly, the third fraction of ML-1 when dispersed in water exhibits new peak at 600–610 nm (SF 8 f-h). Moreover, precipitate was formed upon addition of water. Fluorescence QY serves to assess the ability of a molecule to emit photons after absorption of light of specific wavelength [21,22]. The ML-1 displayed a QY of 6.68 %, whereas the third fraction of ML-1 shows more than 2-fold increase valuing to 13.48 %. As the third fraction of ML-1 showed a unique higher wavelength emission and double the QY as that of the ML-1, further studies were performed using ML-1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LC-MS and NMR studies of constituent fractions of ML-1
ce:section.ce:para: During solvothermal reaction of CDs, products and by-products are formed [23]. Therefore, the extent of separation or the presence of precursor in the fractions obtained after column separation of ML-1 was assessed by LC-MS analysis. The first fraction upon injection to the column separates further into fourteen components as evident from the chromatogram presented in Fig. 2 a in 4.51 mins. All the 14 separated fractions were analysed using the mass spectrometer (SF 9). The mass spectra did not show a peak at m/z = 230.2, which indicated the absence of the precursor PCA. However, the chromatogram obtained for second fraction display numerous peaks indicating presence of several by-products (Fig. 2 b). The complete separation of the second fraction happened at a longer duration of 14.86 mins. The corresponding mass spectra (SF 10) were also acquired, and the fraction separated after retention time of 10.69 mins (mass spectrum: peak ID 67) indicated the presence of the precursor. Interestingly, the third fraction however presented a single peak LC chromatogram (Fig. 2 c) with a retention time of 5 mins, indicating the presence of a single species and absence of PCA (Fig. 2 d) or any by-products.

In addition to LC-MS analysis, 13C NMR was used to study the chemical groups present in ML-1 and column purified fractions. The 13C NMR spectrum of ML-1 exhibits peaks at 17.95–18.55 and 56.41–56.99 ppm belonging to ethanol (SF 11a). The spectrum shows peaks at 193.64 ppm and 122.91–131.50 ppm (marked region) for the presence of carbonyl and aromatic carbons in addition to few peaks for aliphatic carbons. The peaks observed at 11.25–30.32 ppm (marked region in the inset of SF 11a) indicate the presence of sp3 hybridized carbon. Unlike ML-1 sample, the first (SF 11b) and the second fractions (SF 11c) do not possess carbonyl group as evident from their respective NMR spectra. The third fraction (SF 11d) displays peaks corresponding to carbonyl and aromatic carbons (193.74 ppm and 122.88–135.46 ppm) indicating their abundant presence in ML-1 in comparison to first and second fractions. Thus, the LC-MS and 13C NMR data suggest the presence of a single species in the third fraction eluted during the column separation of ML-1.

Based on the prominent solvatochromism property and higher QY value, the third fraction of ML-1 was subjected to further characterisation. Initially to analyse the morphological features, the third fraction was drop casted on copper grid to view the presence of quasi-spherical CDs as portrayed in Fig. 3 a having average diameter of 8–12 nm (Fig. 3 b). The SAED pattern (Fig. 3 c) obtained over a specific area includes distinct ring indicating polycrystallinity of CDs. Moreover, the d-space values of the diffraction patterns were measured as 0.31, 0.21 and 0.12 nm. The HRTEM depicted in Fig. 3 d shows the lattice infringes value estimated between the orderly arrangement of lattice points which matches with one of the diffraction patterns (d = 0.21 nm). The HAADF image presented in Fig. 3 e specifies the elemental distribution. The elemental mapping shows Carbon (C) and Oxygen (O) (Fig. 3 f and g). Furthermore, the TEM-EDS spectrum as shown in Fig. 3 h presents similar findings.

The XRD spectrum of the CDs in third fraction as shown in Fig. 4 a was recorded by drop casting the sample on a glass slide. A highly intense diffraction peak was observed at 9.69° in addition to less intense peaks (inset image of Fig. 4 a) at 12.33°, 12.88°, 17.75°, 19.17°, 24° and 28.7°. The d-space values of the corresponding diffraction patterns were calculated in accordance with Bragg’s equation (nλ = 2dsinθ, where ‘n’ refers to the order of diffraction of 1, λ is the source wavelength of 1.540562 Å, d is the space between atomic planes and θ is the diffraction beam angle). The estimated d-space values for corresponding 2θ values are listed in the table Supplementary Table (ST) 1.

The Raman spectra of PCA, ethanol and the CDs in third fraction were recorded for excitation at 785 nm to gather insights on different chemical groups (Fig. 4 b). The precursor exhibited peaks at 1243 cm−1 and 1386 &1409 cm−1 attributed to CH in-plane bend and C–C stretch, while peaks at 1598 cm−1 and 1627 cm−1 are due to aromatic CC stretching vibration in addition to CO stretch at 1674 cm−1 [24–26]. The Raman spectrum for ethanol acquired using quartz plate displayed several characteristic peaks. The peaks that appeared from 1513 to 1800 cm−1 are from quartz plate (marked in black), whereas those observed at 1276 cm−1 and 1457 cm−1 could be due to both the CH2 deformation and wagging mode vibrations. In addition, the peak at 1483 cm−1 could be attributed to the CH3 anti-symmetric weak mode deformation vibration [25,27–29]. The Raman spectrum of CDs displayed similar peaks to that of PCA indicating the presence of similar chemical groups. The Raman peaks at 1229 and 1409 cm−1 are due to CH in-plane bend and C–C stretch. Moreover, peaks at 1594 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1 result from aromatic CC stretching vibration. The peak at 1674 cm−1 could be due to CO stretching vibration mode. Additionally, the peaks at 1386 cm−1 and 1508 cm−1 could be assigned to D and G band [30]. The values of Raman shift (cm−1) for PCA, ethanol and CDs are listed in ST 2.

The FT-IR spectra of ML-1 and CDs are compared to that of solid PCA as showcased in Fig. 4 c. The several peaks observed for the precursor between 697 and 1062 cm−1 are attributed to C-H in plane deformations while peaks ranging between 1172 and 1369 cm−1 result from C-C in-plane-bending vibrations. The peaks spanning the 1496–1589 cm−1 region are due to CC stretch. The distinct CO stretching band is observed at 1676 cm−1 [24,25]. In case of ML-1 and CDs the spectra are recorded in ethanol and methanol, respectively. The appearance of peak at 882–1045 cm−1 and 882–1027 cm−1 in ML-1 and CDs respectively are due to C-H in plane deformations [31]. The ML-1 spectrum exhibited peaks at 1380 cm−1 for CH3 wagging mode and 1670 cm−1 for CO stretch. The CDs displayed characteristic peaks at 1403 cm−1 and 1660 cm−1 for CH3 bend and CO stretching vibrations. The FT-IR spectra of both ML-1 and CDs display peaks at 2834–2880 cm−1 and 2973–2976 cm−1 corresponding to C-H symmetric and asymmetric stretch. The ST 3 lists the FT-IR peak (cm−1) values of PCA, ML-1 and CDs.

XPS analysis provides insights into the elemental composition and corresponding hybridisation status of CDs in the third fraction. The acquired survey scan spectrum (Fig. 4 d) depicts peaks at 285.2 and 532.3 eV corresponding to C1s and O1s mainly from the sample [32]. Moreover, peaks at 168.3, 349.8 and 1073.0 eV indicate the presence of S2p, Ca2p and Na1s due to glass substrate. In addition, the auger electron for Na and C corresponding to KLL subshell is evident from the peaks at 500.6 and 977.6 eV. The estimated elemental composition illustrates C (50.16 %) and O (35.22 %). Deconvolution was performed on full scan spectrum of C1s to fit three peaks (Fig. 4 e). The fitted peaks at 284.5, 286.0 and 289.1 eV are assigned to C-C/CC, C-O and CO. The similar deconvolution on the full scan spectrum of O1s (Fig. 4 f) enables to fit two peaks at 532.0 and 536.3 eV for CO and C-O group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aggregation induced emission studies and detection of water in alcohol
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence in CDs is reported to rely on size-dependent emission (quantum confinement effect) and surface state (surface passivation). The CDs that are highly fluorescent in dispersive state may exhibit weak or no fluorescence in aggregate or solid state (ACQ). On the contrary, the CDs that possess weak emission in dispersive state can display enhanced fluorescence in aggregated state (AIE). The decline or enhancement in the fluorescence properties is governed by π-π stacking of the CDs; it is assumed that aromatic groups contribute significantly to the PL features of the CDs [8,36,37]. As the third fraction of ML-1 dispersed in water not only exhibits a new peak at 600–610 nm, but also formation of precipitate, studies on aggregation tendency of the CDs of third fraction of ML-1 was performed in THF-water solvent system with different water percentages (0−100). A few aliquots of CDs are dispersed initially in THF and gradually water content is increased to maintain a total volume of 5 ML. The solutions were viewed under daylight and UV illumination ( Fig. 5 a). The PL spectrum of CDs dispersed in 50–90 % water onwards exhibits 10 nm shifts for excitation at 420 nm (Fig. 5 b). Moreover, there is significant increase in PL intensity till 70 % water content and further addition of water does not induce any increment as evident from the bar graph presented in inset of Fig. 5 b. In addition, the PL plots acquired for CDs dispersed in 0–70 % water display emissions at 490 and 530 nm for excitations at 440, 450 and 470 nm (SF 12a-c). Further addition of water (80 % and 100 %) induces additional PL emission peaks from 540 to 700 nm as shown in SF 12a-b. Introduction of water induces the formation of precipitates when CDs are dispersed in 90 % water (10 % THF). Furthermore, the formed precipitate was collected, dried, and 3D PL spectra was recorded, which displayed major emissions confined between 450 and 580 nm (Fig. 5 c). The precipitates display prominent emission at 540 nm for excitations at 370 and 430 nm (Fig. 5 d) depicting different emissive features compared to CDs.

To confirm the structural changes induced upon addition of water, DEPT 135 spectra were recorded in DMSO-d6. The spectrum for CDs dispersed in 0 % water (SF 12d) exhibits solvent peaks at 39.42–40.26 ppm, while the downward peaks at 25.31–25.65 and 67.25–67.58 ppm belong to –CH2 group in THF. The upward peaks at 122.97–131.53 indicate the presence of aromatic carbon in CDs. However, the addition of water causes the disappearance of these peaks (region marked in red in DEPT-135 and 13C NMR spectra presented in SF 12e-f) belonging to aromatic carbon. This is associated with the formation of aggregates because of π-π stacking, thereby resulting in the enhancement of PL intensity [38]. The SEM image obtained for CDs dispersed in 100 % THF (0 % water) displays the nanoparticles (Fig. 5 e). However, the micro image of CDs dispersed in 100 % water reveal visible aggregate formation (Fig. 5 f). The X-ray diffraction plots of CDs in solutions of 100 % THF and 100 % water, respectively exhibit sharp peaks indicating crystalline nature (SF 12 g). The d-space values estimated for CDs in 100 % water (ST 5 and ST 6) did not display any changes in comparison to 100 % THF. Therefore, the AIE phenomenon exhibited by CDs is due to the interactions of surface functional groups with the solvent.

The AIE phenomenon displayed by the CDs was further validated via time resolved fluorescence measurements. In the absence of water (100 % THF), CDs displayed multi-exponential decay (SF 13a) for emissions at 490 nm (1.18 and 0.32 ns) and 530 nm (1.17 and 0.33 ns) for excitations at 440 and 450 nm. The CDs dispersed in water exhibited additional emissions at 540, 550 and 560 nm; their average lifetime values were also determined. The lifetime plots for CDs dispersed in 100 % water recorded at 440, 450 and 470 nm excitations (SF 13b-d), display multi-exponential decays. In addition, marked increase in average lifetime values of 1.28, 2.14 and 1.96 ns for emissions at 540, 550 and 560 nm for excitation at 440 nm was observed. Thus, the introduction of water in CDs increases both PL intensity and fluorescence decay rates. The individual and average lifetime values of CDs dispersed in 0 % and 100 % water are presented in ST 7 and ST 8.

Since CDs form visible aggregates in water, they were explored to detect the presence of water in ethanol and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) samples. Varying ratios of ethanol-water and IPA-water (varying water content in the mixture) were prepared and 200 µL of CD dispersion was added. The photographs of the respective solutions in daylight and under 365 nm UV illumination are shown in Fig. 6 a and 6 d. The CDs dispersed in alcohol display emissions at 450–470 nm and 530–540 nm (SF 14a-d and Figs. 6 b-c, 6 e-f) indicating prominent blue fluorescence. However, the CDs in 0–30 % ethanol and 0–20 % IPA have spectral shift to 580 nm (ST 9 & 10), respectively. Moreover, the CDs dispersion in alcohol (ethanol or IPA) that appear fluorescent blue changes to fluorescent yellow upon increasing water content. This can serve as a fluorescence-based assay to check the presence of water in alcohol samples. To further understand the interactions of CDs with alcohol, Raman spectra were recorded (SF 14e-f), which showed characteristic peak of CDs at 1594 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1 belonging to aromatic carbon, in addition to carbonyl peak at 1674 cm−1. In the spectrum of CDs dispersed in water medium, these peaks are not observed. However, these characteristic peaks are observed when CDs are dispersed in alcohol-water system. The Raman shift (cm−1) peaks for CDs alone and dispersed in alcohol-water system are listed in ST 11, ST 12 and ST 13. The CIE plots mimic the way colours are perceived by naked eye. The presence of water in alcohols (ethanol and IPA) induces major shift towards longer wavelength region (SF 15a-b). The AIE phenomenon of PCA-CDs in water governed by the formation of aggregates resulting in prominent red-shift could thus serve in detection of water in alcoholic samples. Moreover, evaluation of CIE plots can be performed to sense the presence of water in alcohol samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ink formulations and evaluation of print properties
ce:section.ce:para: Solvent based ink formulations are widely used as their prints require lesser drying time. However, one of the major concerns is the formation of volatile organic compounds either during manufacturing, blending, or printing process of the ink. Water based formulations can be used as an alternative as they are eco-friendly and have better wettability properties. Two water-based inks were formulated using the third fraction CDs of ML-1 with PVA and HEC as binders and the print properties as well as security applications of the printed proofs were explored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PVA based screen ink and print properties
ce:section.ce:para: PVA was chosen as the binder for the water-based screen ink due to its better film forming properties upon drying. Initially few granules of PVA were dissolved in distilled water and heated at 90℃ for 1 h with stirring. This ensures complete dissolution to obtain homogenous PVA binder solution. Surfactant (OT 75) was added to ease the dissolution process of CDs dispersion, and subsequently defoamer (Tego foamex 1488) to counter the effect of foaming. The Table 1 provides the composition in weight percentage of PVA based screen ink.

The 3D PL spectrum ( Fig. 7 a) recorded for the ink exhibits major emissions at 460 nm for excitations at 310, 370 and 420 nm (marked as A, B and C). The effect of ink components on the emission profile of the CDs was monitored by recording the emission responses at 440, 450 and 470 nm excitations (Fig. 7 b), and the peaks centred at 490 and 540 nm emissions belong to CDs alone. This indicated that the presence of additives did not affect the fluorescence of CDs. Prior to screen printing, the ink was coated on UV dull paper via draw down technique using K-bar coater. The coated sample appeared pale yellow under daylight and fluorescent blue under UV light (SF 16a). The ink coated paper exhibited emissions at 470 and 535 nm in addition to a new emission band at 580 nm for excitations at 370 and 400 nm as presented in Fig. 7 c. To confirm the emissions from the inked region alone, the emissions from UV dull paper alone (SF 17a) was measured, which appeared at 440–530 nm and 470–530 for excitations at 370 and 400 nm. The emission beyond 530 nm thus belongs to CDs coated on the UV dull paper. Furthermore, the similar emission profiles of CDs are observed by the usage of excitations at 440, 450 and 470 nm (Fig. 7 d).

Screen print proofs were obtained for single and multiple layers which appears fluorescent blue under UV source. The PL spectrum of screen print proofs displayed emissions at 450, 530 and 570 nm for excitations at 370, 390, 400, 410 and 420 nm (SF 17b). The CDs in screen printed proofs exhibit peaks at 470, 530 and 570 nm for excitations at 440, 450 and 470 nm indicating the interactions of CDs with UV dull paper. The emissions beyond 530 nm are mainly from the CDs. The photostability of the screen print was investigated by passing 1.5 cm × 1.5 cm printed proofs into the UV curing unit at the rate of 300 W/30 m/min for 10, 20, 30 and 40 passes. The exposed print proofs displayed cyan fluorescence as shown in SF 16b. As evident from the overlay PL spectra of UV light unexposed and exposed print proofs, there is no major decline in intensity (Fig. 7 e-f & SF 17c-h). However, greater the extent of UV exposure duration or cycles of the print proofs, greater is the PL intensity. Further, the print was kept in UV chamber (λex = 365 nm) of 200 W power for 60 mins (SF 17c-h), to observe a rise in PL intensity in comparison to spectra of unexposed print. Thus, the use of PVA as binder offers protection against photo-bleaching effect on CDs.

The wear resistance test of print proofs was tested using Rub tester to evaluate the response of print against brushing or scuffling effect. For this experiment, print proofs were cut into two halves and placed on rubber pad which is mounted on basal plate. Then 2 lbs weight block was placed, and abrasion resistance of the ink film was tested for 50 and 100 rubs. The PL spectra of 50 and 100 rubs print (SF 17i-j) proofs did not show any significant decline in PL intensity. This proves the stability of print against peeling effect upon application of mechanical force. PVA has numerous -OH groups which can effectively engage in hydrogen bonding with the cellulosic paper substrate, which enables good adherence of the print.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HEC based screen ink and evaluation of print properties
ce:section.ce:para: HEC is a water-soluble polymer used as a gelling agent. Due to its property to form wide range of viscous solutions and better adherence property they are widely used in coating applications in paper industry. Therefore, the CDs were used to prepare screen ink using HEC as binder and its print properties were studied. The HEC granules were dissolved in hot water with continuous stirring to prepare the binder for the screen ink. After subsequent cooling, the CDs dispersion was added to the binder solution and stirred to obtain a homogenous solution. Additives were not used in this formulation as HEC exhibits gelling property and the formulated ink exhibited minimal foaming. The final screen ink had a composition of (weight %) 26.18 % CDs dispersion (pigment concentrate) and 73.82 % binder. The HEC based screen ink appears yellow under daylight and show bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. The PL contour plot for screen ink have major emissions at 470 and 530 nm ( Fig. 8 a) for excitations at 370 and 400 nm. The PL peaks of CDs remain unaltered when excited at 440, 450 and 470 nm as evident from the PL plot (Fig. 8 b) indicating its inertness towards the binder.

The HEC-based ink coats on UV dull paper were invisible under daylight and exhibit blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV illumination (SF 18a). The coats displayed maximum emission at 450 nm (marked as A and B) for excitations at 370 and 400 nm (Fig. 8 c). In addition, a shoulder peak at 530 nm was observed for similar excitations (Fig. 8 d). The screen-printed proofs also displayed emissions at 440–450 nm and 530 nm for similar excitations (SF 19a). To distinguish emissions from binder, a control formulation was prepared, and screen printed on UV dull paper. The control screen print proof exhibited PL peaks at 440–460 and 530 nm (SF 19b). It can be inferred that emissions from screen ink overlaps with that from HEC binder. The PL spectra for the un/exposed print proofs (SF 18b) to UV curing unit at the rate of 300 W/30 m/min was recorded and compared (Fig. 8 e-f & SF 19c-g). The print proof exhibited stability up to 20 passes, whereas a significant decline in the PL intensity was observed when exposed to 30 and 40 passes due to photo-bleaching. However, the duration of UV exposure (60 mins) does not have significant impact on the PL intensity of print proofs. The abrasion resistance test (SF 19 h-i) performed on prints reveals its tendency to peel off upon application of mechanical force. The surface morphology of the screen-printed proofs using both PVA and HEC-based inks showcases uniform distribution of the ink on the surface of UV dull paper; better coating in case of PVA-based CD ink (SF 20a-d).

The International Commission on Illumination recommends the use of 3D CIE colour spaces which mimics the colour perceived by human eye and is used to monitor the colour of printed samples. The L*, a* and b* values are used to describe the colour of the printed proofs. The L* values that range between 0 and 100 represent the whiteness of the sample. The chromatic parameters a* and b* values lie between −120–120. The negative and positive values of a* corresponds to green and red colour, whereas the negative and positive values of b* establishes blue and yellow colour of a printed sample. Another colour parameter used to describe the print is ΔE; if the value is >2, the print is visible. However, if the values of ΔE lie between 0 and 2, the print is invisible and can only be analysed by colour specialist.

The UV dull paper used as printing substrate in the present study has L*, a*, b* and density values measured as 91.51, −0.3, 2.67 and 0.1. The L*, a* and b* values determined for PVA based screen print are 92.97, −1.95 and 6.55. The closeness of L* values to 100 indicates the whiteness of the print proofs. The negative and positive values of a* and b* signify colour spaces for green and yellow. On subsequent UV passes, a* value decreases (more negative) and b* value increases. However, the L* values of the print remain closer to 100 indicating the print colour is unaffected after UV exposure. The value of ΔE > 2 signifies that the print is perceivable to human eye. The abrasion tested print exhibited minimal changes in L*, a* and b* values suggesting good colour stability after rubbing.

The colorimetric values (L*, a* and b*) of HEC based screen print are 93.58, −0.83 and 2.54. There are no considerable differences in L*, a* and b* values for UV light exposed and unexposed print proofs. Abrasion tested samples also exhibited only minor variations in colorimetric values. The ΔE values for UV exposed and abrasion tested prints are between 0 and 2 as they are invisible to naked eye. The graphical representation (SF 21a-d) and ST 14–17 depict the colorimetric and density values of screen prints using PVA and HEC based inks after UV exposure cycles and abrasion resistance test.

Security inks display desirable covert features that enable easy differentiation between genuine and counterfeit products. Hong and Yang utilized wasted coffee grounds to synthesize blue emissive CDs. The formulated ink when sprayed on bank notes, appear invisible under daylight and exhibits distinct fluorescence under UV illumination [39]. Similarly, security inks are prepared and printed on paper and cloth materials to exhibit covert features [40–43]. In another report, the ink formulation was printed on filter paper and the inscribed QR code showed green fluorescence under UV light and further shifted to cyan emission upon application of a base. Moreover, the green fluorescence re-appeared upon addition of dilute acetic acid [44]. The present work involves exploration of unique anti-counterfeiting features of PVA binder-based print proofs, which are superior to HEC binder based print proofs. The PVA binder-based print displayed blue and cyan fluorescence under excitations at 370, 410, 440 and 450 nm ( Fig. 9 a). Interestingly, the authenticity of the print is validated using UV block filters which can enable the user to view greenish yellow fluorescence under similar excitations. The unprinted UV dull paper is viewed under similar excitations wherein it exhibits majorly violet and blue emissions, thereby eliminating contributions from UV dull paper (SF 22). The visualization of specific fluorescence is realised only in PVA based formulation and not in the case of HEC binder-based ink (Fig. 9 b). The use of two different inks in inscribing specific data is confidential and difficult to replicate. As shown in Fig. 9 c, the word ‘MIT’ is inscribed using PVA based ink whereas the word ‘MANIPAL’ is written using HEC based ink. Under daylight both the letters are invisible. Under UV source, the word ‘MIT’ appears bright Cyan fluorescent, while the word ‘MANIPAL’ exhibits fluorescent blue. Interestingly, while using UV block filter, ‘MIT’ possesses distinct yellow fluorescence whereas there are no obvious changes in the fluorescence colour emitted by ‘MANIPAL’. This can serve as an additional authentication feature while using PVA based security ink.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages of PVA binder: print properties and security features
ce:section.ce:para: The use of PVA as binder in screen ink formulation offers stability and multi-level authenticating features to the print proofs. PVA based proofs exhibited resistance to photobleaching and mechanical force. However, the HEC based print displayed stability only up to few UV pass cycles and lesser resistance to abrasion test. Security print with multi-level features is desirable as it makes the duplication of counterfeit products difficult. The authenticity of PVA print can be realised by the usage of UV block filters which can essentially block blue emissions. Thus, the forgers observe the blue fluorescence from the prints, whereas the users can perceive the yellow fluorescence and authenticate the print under commonly available 365 nm light source. Notably, the use of such formulations can be printed on paper substrates using simple screen-printing technique.

The CDs prepared from PCA via solvothermal approach and subsequent purification using column chromatography displayed emissions at 490 and 535 nm with a fluorescence QY of 13.48 %. Moreover, a red shifted aggregation induced emission was observed when PCA-CDs were dispersed in THF-water mixtures with increased water content. The NMR plots for such systems indicate the absence of peaks corresponding to aromatic carbon due to π-π stacking. Moreover, the separated aggregates exhibited similar emissions as that of CDs and possessed dimensions beyond nanoparticle range. The time resolved fluorescence measurement indicated increment in lifetime values for aggregates in water medium. The addition of water in alcohol induced enhancement in the fluorescence of CDs due to their aggregation and not via changes in their structural conformations. The PCA-derived CDs could be utilised for fluorescence-based detection of water content in alcohol samples. The CDs were incorporated in two water based screen ink formulations with PVA and HEC as binders. The screen print proofs on UV dull paper using PVA based ink demonstrated better photostability and abrasion resistance in comparison to that printed using HEC based ink. The emission profiles of PVA based print proofs have a prominent peak at 570 nm whereas HEC based print have emissions confined at 450–530 nm. Additionally, though the PVA based print apparently appears blue or cyan fluorescent (λem = 470 nm), the yellow emissions (λem = 580 nm) can be visualised using UV block filter which can effectively cut-off short wavelength component. Such features, masked to the forger, but known to the user can be utilised in checking the authenticity of the print.
